page: Amazon Web Services m6a.xlarge is a general-purpose server instance powered by the AWS Nitro hypervisor. It features an AMD EPYC 7R13 processor with an x86_64 architecture, operating at a clock speed of 3.6 GHz. The instance is configured with 4 dedicated vCPUs derived from 2 physical cores with hyperthreading enabled, providing 2 threads per core. It includes 16.0 GB of DDR4 memory running at 3 GHz, which equates to 4.0 GB of memory per core. This instance does not include local storage or dedicated GPU hardware, and it offers a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps.

Benchmark data reveals a divergence between single-core and multi-core performance. The instance demonstrates strong single-core CPU performance in stress-ng tests, ranking in the top 25%. However, multi-core performance is weaker, falling into the bottom 25% for both stress-ng and Geekbench multi-core tests. Memory bandwidth is also a bottleneck, with both read and write operations for large blocks categorized in the bottom 25%. Cryptographic and physics calculations show average performance, while database operations, Redis SET operations, and static web serving throughput are relatively weak.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the m6a.xlarge offers a balanced resource allocation profile for general-purpose workloads that do not require high-throughput local storage or intensive multi-threaded processing. Given the weak multi-core scaling and low memory bandwidth, it is less suitable for heavy database operations, large-scale software compilation, or real-time image processing. It is best utilized for lightweight web serving, basic text processing, single-threaded applications, and small-scale model inference where single-core performance is the primary driver.
description: Amazon Web Services m6a.xlarge is a general-purpose instance powered by the AWS Nitro hypervisor and an AMD EPYC 7R13 processor running at 3.6 GHz. It features 4 dedicated vCPUs, 16.0 GB of DDR4 RAM, and a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps, with no local storage or GPU. Benchmarks indicate strong single-core CPU performance, placing in the top 25% in stress-ng tests. However, multi-core performance, memory bandwidth, and database operations fall into the bottom 25%. This resource profile makes the instance qualitatively cost-effective for workloads that rely heavily on single-threaded execution rather than parallel processing. It is suited for lightweight web serving, text processing, and small-scale model inference, but is less optimal for data-intensive databases or multi-threaded compilation tasks.
og_description: Amazon Web Services m6a.xlarge is a general-purpose instance featuring 4 vCPUs, 16 GB DDR4 RAM, and an AMD EPYC 7R13 CPU. It offers strong single-core performance but exhibits weaker multi-core and memory bandwidth.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services m6a.xlarge is a general-purpose instance with 4 vCPUs, 16 GB RAM, and an AMD EPYC CPU, offering strong single-core performance.
tagline: A general-purpose instance featuring dedicated AMD EPYC processing, delivering strong single-core performance with a balanced memory-to-vCPU ratio.
bullet_points: Powered by an AMD EPYC 7R13 processor at 3.6 GHz with 4 dedicated vCPUs
Equipped with 16.0 GB of DDR4 memory and 2 Gbps baseline network bandwidth
Strong single-core CPU performance ranking in the top 25% of benchmarks
Weaker multi-core scaling and memory bandwidth under heavy parallel workloads
No local storage or GPU accelerators included in the hardware profile
Best suited for single-threaded applications, text processing, and light web serving
